mnemonics.title: PROCUREMENT Steps
mnemonics.content:
P -lan for Requirements
R -esearch Vendors
O -btain Proposals (RFP)
C -ompare and Evaluate
U -pon Select Vendor
R -eview Contract
E -xecute Order
M -anage Implementation
E -valuate Performance
N -egotiate Future Deals
T -erminate or Renew

acronyms.usage: Used to evaluate true cost of IT procurement including purchase, operation, and disposal
Document sent to vendors to obtain proposals for IT requirements
Contract defining expected service standards and penalties
Commercial document authorizing a purchase transaction
When IT hardware/software is no longer supported by vendor

visualTips.visualization: Draw as a circular flow: Requirement → Identify Suppliers → Evaluate → Contract → Order → Implement → Manage → Review. Show arrows returning to Requirement for continuous improvement.

Create a layered triangle: Top layer (visible costs) = Purchase price, Installation; Middle layer = Maintenance, Training; Bottom layer (hidden costs) = Downtime, Upgrades, Disposal. Show how bottom layer often exceeds top.

Three-column table: Purchase (High upfront, Low ongoing, Own asset) | Lease (Medium upfront, Medium ongoing, Option to own) | Rental/Cloud (Low/No upfront, Pay-per-use, No ownership). Show trade-off arrows.

analogies.analogy: Like planning a wedding: Identify needs (find partner) → Research venues/vendors → Evaluate options → Book (contract) → Organize event → Manage the day → Review photos (performance review).

Like being locked into a phone contract - switching carriers requires buying new phone, losing number, and paying termination fees. Similarly, changing IT vendors can require retraining, data migration, and contract penalties.
Like buying a car - the sticker price is just the beginning. You also need insurance, fuel, maintenance, repairs, and eventual disposal costs. TCO for IT includes all these hidden costs.
Like a warranty or guarantee - if the product doesn't work as promised, you get your money back or compensation. SLA promises specific service levels with penalties for failure.
